macro_events.event_summary: Iran reportedly proposed a deal to the U.S. regarding the Strait of Hormuz, signaling potential de-escalation in the Persian Gulf, though talks remain stalled.
primary_drivers: Reduced geopolitical risk premium in crude oil if deal progresses
Lower probability of Hormuz shipping disruption (~20% of global oil flows)
Compression of safe-haven bid in Treasuries/gold

beneficiaries.rationale: Jet fuel is ~25-30% of operating costs; any easing of crude on Hormuz de-escalation directly expands airline margins. Delta has highest jet fuel sensitivity among legacy carriers post-hedge roll-off.
Same jet fuel cost mechanism as DAL; United has heavier international/long-haul exposure where fuel is a larger share of trip cost, amplifying the leverage to a crude pullback.
Bunker fuel is a major variable cost for cruise operators; a Hormuz risk-premium unwind lowers Brent and bunker prices, plus reduces Middle East itinerary disruption risk.
Naphtha/ethane feedstock costs track crude; commodity chemical margins expand on a geopolitical risk-premium unwind. Direct input cost pass-through.

losers.rationale: Integrated major with significant upstream leverage to Brent; a Hormuz de-escalation compresses the geopolitical risk premium (~$5-10/bbl) directly hitting upstream realizations. Largest US-listed pure beneficiary of Gulf tension reversing.
High-beta US E&P with heavy Permian crude leverage; oil price decline on risk-off-the-table headlines hits cash flow and buyback capacity disproportionately vs. peers.
Smaller-cap E&P with direct WTI/Brent price sensitivity; high beta to crude moves makes it a clean short-vehicle for de-escalation trade.
Oilfield services activity correlates with sustained crude prices; reduced geopolitical premium pressures upstream capex outlook and rig count expectations.
Product tanker rates spike on Hormuz disruption fears (longer ton-mile re-routing); de-escalation removes the war-risk premium and could compress spot tanker rates.
Crude tanker operator directly leveraged to Hormuz disruption pricing; VLCC rates incorporate war-risk insurance premium that unwinds on de-escalation news.
Defense primes carry a Middle East conflict premium; sustained de-escalation headlines pressure the sector even though backlog is unaffected near-term. Sentiment-driven derate.

headlines: Iran reportedly proposes Hormuz Strait deal to U.S. Here’s where things stand — and what’s next for markets
